appearance.title: Appearance
appearance.description: Change the visual theme of the application

step1.title: Start on Home
step1.body: Click "Tutorial" whenever you need context. On Home, scroll down to understand what an asynchronous API is, what a broker does, and which path a test follows.
details: Backstage is the web app.
AKS is the Azure cluster where tests run.
Results is where you check if the test went well.

step2.title: Check the catalog
step2.body: The catalog shows which brokers, protocols, and formats you can test.
details: Open a card if you are not sure what a component does.
Compatibility helps avoid combinations that do not make sense.

step3.title: Create the scenario
step3.body: In Scenarios you choose the combination to test: broker, architecture, protocol, and data format.
details: Choose broker, architecture, protocol, and format.
The format changes message size and recommended load.

step4.title: Follow the run
step4.body: Executions shows whether a test is pending, running, completed, or failed.
details: Filter to find the run quickly.
Open the detail if you want live data.

step5.title: Read Results
step5.body: Results compares tests and explains the score.
details: Do not look only at the final number.
Open the detail to see the calculation and errors.

step1.title: Filter the catalog
step1.body: Use filters to quickly find brokers, protocols, or formats.
details: Filter before creating a test.
Search helps when you do not remember the exact name.

step2.title: Open a card
step2.body: Click any catalog row to open the component sheet. Inside it, click "Reproducibility" to see how to repeat the test and "Configuration" for useful commands.
details: Read the short description.
Check if it fits the test you want to run.

step3.title: Check compatibility
step3.body: Not every combination makes sense. This area helps you avoid mistakes.
details: Check broker, protocol, and architecture.
If an adapter is needed, it should be clear.

step4.title: Check configuration
step4.body: The detail separates overview, configuration, and reproducibility.
details: Review versions and ports.
Copy snippets only when you need them.

step5.title: Go to Scenarios
step5.body: When the component fits, use it as a base for the test.
details: You do not need to memorize everything.
The catalog is the starting point.

step1.title: Create a scenario
step1.body: Click New scenario to open the form.
details: Use a clear name.
The name should say broker, format, and goal.

step2.title: Choose the composition
step2.body: This is the important choice: broker, architecture, and protocol.
details: Choose the platform first.
Then check that protocol and architecture fit together.

step3.title: Choose the format
step3.body: The data format changes payload, rate, and how the score should be read.
details: IoT is not the same load as video.
If you change payload by hand, check that the broker supports it.

step4.title: Use presets
step4.body: Presets are prepared examples. They are good for starting quickly.
details: Use one when you want a safe test.
You can adjust it afterwards.

step5.title: Run or duplicate
step5.body: In the list you can run, duplicate, edit, or delete.
details: Duplicate to change only one variable.
Run when the setup is clear.

step6.title: Set duration and warm-up
step6.body: Warm-up lets the system settle before measuring.
details: Avoid runs that are too short.
A stable test gives more useful results.

step1.title: Filter executions
step1.body: Click the state, platform, protocol, architecture, format, or scenario filters. Scenario is last because state and platform are usually the first filters you need.
details: Filter by status when there are many tests.
Filter by broker or format when comparing one thing.

step2.title: Open the detail
step2.body: Click a row to see the run setup and status.
details: The detail should not close by itself.
Check duration, format, and status before reading metrics.

step3.title: Watch live data
step3.body: When a run is active, this is where incoming data appears.
details: High errors mean trouble.
Stable throughput is a good sign.

step4.title: Stop if needed
step4.body: You can stop a test if it is wrong or too heavy for the cluster.
details: Stopping keeps partial data when available.
Deleting removes the run from the view.

step5.title: Go to Results
step5.body: When the run ends, saved data appears in Results.
details: Do not decide from live data only.
Wait for persisted data.

step1.title: Filter before comparing
step1.body: Click the data format filter first. It is the cleanest comparison because IoT, financial, and video use different loads.
details: Compare similar things.
Changing filters can change the visible score.

step2.title: Read the metrics
step2.body: Latency, throughput, and errors explain what happened.
details: A high score with errors is not reliable.
Check the format before judging.

step3.title: Open a row
step3.body: Click a row to see the scenario detail.
details: You see which runs contributed.
You also see total messages and measures.

step4.title: Understand the score
step4.body: The score adds weighted metrics. The detail shows the calculation.
details: You see normalized value times weight.
You also see the error penalty.

step5.title: Compare two scenarios
step5.body: Comparison is most useful when you change only one thing.
details: Change a broker or a format, not everything at once.
That makes the conclusion clearer.

home.title: Async APIs
home.subtitle: Comparable tests, clear results
home.description: This portal explains and compares asynchronous APIs with real benchmark runs. Start here: learn the concepts, review the catalog, create scenarios, execute runs, and read the results.
home.heroSubtitle: Backstage is the portal screen. AKS is the Azure cluster where the test containers run.

guide.title: Quick orientation guide
guide.subtitle: Home gives the context: what an asynchronous API is, what a broker does, which pages exist, and where to click first.

map.title: Where to start
map.text: Read the blocks in order: concepts, system parts, message flow, test cycle, and portal pages.

catalog.title: Start with components
catalog.text: Click "View catalog" or "Catalog" in the top navigation to see components, versions, compatibility, and reproducibility.

scenarios.title: Then create the test
scenarios.text: Click "Create scenario" or "Scenarios" to choose platform, architecture, protocol, data format, and load.

results.title: Finish with data
results.text: Click "Executions" to inspect run state and "Results" to interpret measures, score, and comparisons.

guide.tutorialHintText: click the Tutorial button above to open the guided walkthrough. Each step says exactly which button or tab to click before moving to the next screen.

home.brokerBody: Stores and redistributes messages independently of producers and consumers

sections.systemDesc: The flow is: you define the test, the orchestrator prepares it, AKS runs the load-generator, and Results shows the data. AKS is the Azure cluster where the containers run.
sections.conceptsDesc: Before comparing, identify what you are changing: platform, protocol, architecture, data format, and load.
sections.brokerDesc: A broker receives messages from producers and delivers them to consumers. This lets each side work at its own pace.
sections.executionTitle: What happens when you run a test?
sections.executionDesc: Each execution follows the same path. This lets you compare results without mixing different cases.
sections.metricsLabel: Metrics you will read in Results

systemParts.title: Backstage
systemParts.subtitle: Screen
systemParts.text: The web app you use. Here you create scenarios, launch tests, and open results.

systemParts.title: Backend
systemParts.subtitle: Preparation
systemParts.text: Takes the scenario and turns it into a test that can run.

systemParts.title: AKS
systemParts.subtitle: Azure cluster
systemParts.text: Where the containers run. It starts the broker, load generator, and metrics services.

systemParts.title: Broker
systemParts.subtitle: Measured part
systemParts.text: Kafka, RabbitMQ, NATS, or similar receive the test messages.

systemParts.title: Metrics
systemParts.subtitle: Data
systemParts.text: The system stores latency, messages per second, errors, and counters.

systemParts.title: Results
systemParts.subtitle: Final read
systemParts.text: Here you compare tests and see which combination worked better.

concepts.title: Async API
concepts.text: A system sends a message and does not wait for an immediate answer. The receiver processes it when it can.

concepts.title: Broker
concepts.text: The piece that receives messages and delivers them to consumers. Kafka, RabbitMQ, and NATS play this role.

concepts.title: Protocol and architecture
concepts.text: The protocol says how the message travels. The architecture says how the pieces are arranged.

concepts.title: Format and load
concepts.text: The format sets the message size. The load says how many messages you send and for how long.

executionSteps.label: Create scenario
executionSteps.sub: choose broker, protocol, format, and load

executionSteps.label: Prepare run
executionSteps.sub: the backend checks and prepares the test

executionSteps.label: Run on AKS
executionSteps.sub: AKS starts the containers and sends load

executionSteps.label: Save data
executionSteps.sub: latency, throughput, and errors are recorded

pages.label: Catalog
pages.desc: Components, versions and configuration needed to replicate the tests.

pages.label: Scenarios
pages.desc: Define broker, architecture, protocol, format, load, rate and payload.

pages.label: Executions
pages.desc: Launch runs on the cluster, stop tests and review the status of each execution.

pages.label: Results
pages.desc: Compare latency, throughput, errors and percentiles per execution or scenario.

metrics.title: P99 latency
metrics.text: Shows the slowest messages. It helps you spot spikes that users would notice.

metrics.title: Throughput
metrics.text: Shows how many messages per second the test handles. High is good only if latency and errors stay healthy.

metrics.title: Error rate
metrics.text: Shows the share of messages that fail or do not arrive correctly. If it rises, the result is less trustworthy.

brokerFlow.title: What a broker does
brokerFlow.description: A broker receives messages and sends them to consumers. The test changes the broker, protocol, or format to see what happens.
brokerFlow.producerText: The generator creates messages with the scenario format, size, and rate.
brokerFlow.consumerText: The consumer confirms what arrived. That later lets you calculate errors and performance.

catalog.heading: Component catalog
catalog.subheading: Components, versions and configuration needed to replicate the tests.
catalog.searchPlaceholder: Search by name, version, tag, category or reproducibility
catalog.noComponents: No component found with the current filters.
catalog.tableSubheading: Select a component to see the full card.

categoryDescriptions.architecture: Pattern describing how messages flow within a scenario: via events, queues, logs or streaming.
categoryDescriptions.protocol: Communication rules that specify how messages are sent and received between producer, broker and consumer.
categoryDescriptions.platform: Real broker or platform deployed on the cluster. It is the piece that processes messages and determines results.

categoryImpacts.architecture: Changes the flow shape and can therefore affect latency, decoupling and consumption capacity.
categoryImpacts.protocol: Changes the transport and acknowledgements. Can affect compatibility, errors, latency and message loss.
categoryImpacts.platform: Changes the implementation running on AKS. This is where versions, ports, topology and limits matter for replication.

compatibilityNotes.Kafka: Reference for logs and streaming. When combined with non-Kafka protocols, the scenario must clearly declare the gateway or adapter.
compatibilityNotes.RabbitMQ: Strong in queues, ACKs and flexible routing. The natural platform for AMQP and queue-work tests.
compatibilityNotes.Confluent: Confluent is tested through the cluster Kafka-compatible path. This test does not include Schema Registry, ksqlDB, or Control Center.
compatibilityNotes.NATS Server: Very lightweight for pub/sub and low latency. For large payloads, verify max_payload before launching the benchmark.

modal.noDescriptionLong: This component does not yet have a description in the catalog.
modal.overviewFallback: Component used by the test portal.
modal.impactFallback: Its impact depends on how it is combined inside the scenario.
modal.reproDescription: This tab explains how someone else would repeat the test: which versions to keep, which channel the run creates, which parameters must not change, and which commands help verify it.
modal.noReproRows: This component does not yet have structured reproducibility data.
modal.noConfigSnippet: This component does not yet have a configuration snippet.
modal.useDescription: Open the scenario form with this value preselected when possible.
modal.sectionWhat: What it is and when to use it
modal.sectionCard: How it is applied in the portal
reproChecklist: Use the same component version and deployment.
Repeat the same combination: broker, architecture, protocol and format.
Keep payload, rate, warm-up and duration unchanged.
Create a dedicated run channel: topic, queue, subject or group-id.
modal.configDescription: This tab is not theory: it is the practical check someone would use to verify that the component is deployed or that the scenario contract is repeatable.
modal.configHintText: run the snippet only as a verification step. The comparison still depends on keeping format, payload, rate, warm-up, duration and concurrency unchanged.

compatTable.heading: Compatible combinations
compatTable.subtitle: Which options you can combine while staying within what the portal can run.

compatDetails.portalDescription: This block shows what the portal can test right now. If a combination needs a gateway or extra configuration, it is not shown as directly executable.

stats.possibleCombinations: possible combinations without applying the matrix

compatibility.matrixDescription: Shows what can run now, what needs configuration, and what is not available yet.

reason.supported: The portal can run this combination with the current backend.
reason.requiresConfig: It can be tested, but the broker or cluster configuration must be checked first.
reason.blocked: The portal does not yet have the path needed to run this combination.
reason.gatewayMissing: This protocol needs a gateway or adapter that is not part of the executable flow yet.
reason.kafkaLargePayload: Kafka and the Kafka-compatible endpoint can test large payloads when message limits are configured consistently.
reason.natsLargePayload: NATS needs max_payload of 4 MB or higher to test 8K video without failing.
reason.rabbitLargePayload: RabbitMQ needs size and memory limits checked before testing 8K video.

scenarios.heading: Benchmark scenarios
scenarios.subtitle: Load configurations for testing asynchronous API combinations

format.appliedRecommendations: Recommended rate and payload will be applied

warmup.label: Warm-up
warmup.tooltip: Initial unmeasured period so the system can stabilize

modal.natsWarning: Remember to verify max_payload on the NATS broker before sending large payloads.
modal.validationRequired: Name, platform, architecture and protocol are required.

deleteModal.title: Delete Scenario
deleteModal.message: Are you sure you want to delete this scenario? This action cannot be undone.

executeModal.messageConfirm: Are you sure you want to launch an execution for this scenario?
executeModal.messageRunning: The run is being created on the cluster. Please wait.
executeModal.messageDone: The execution was successfully launched on the cluster.
executeModal.messageError: Could not launch the run. Check the configuration or try again.

guide.title: Quick guide
guide.subtitle: Choose the composition, check the load, and run only when you know what you want to measure.
guide.tipSuffix: as a starting point. They are optimized for common use cases and act as references for understanding the recommended combinations.

structure.title: Scenario structure
structure.desc: Each scenario combines a Platform, a messaging Architecture and a transport Protocol. Select them in order: the platform automatically filters compatible architectures and protocols.

parameters.title: Execution parameters
parameters.desc: Duration controls how long the benchmark runs. Rate is the send rate. Payload is the size of each message. Together they determine the total load on the AKS cluster.

sustained.title: Sustained mode
sustained.desc: Sustained mode replaces the old indefinite mode: it is always capped at one hour and applies a recommended rate and payload based on format, platform, architecture and protocol.

status.title: Queue states
status.desc: Pending means the run is waiting because the concurrency limit is full. The demo has 3 nodes, so only 3 benchmark Jobs enter at once; the rest wait to avoid contaminating metrics. Running means the pod exists and can send samples.

formats.title: Data formats
formats.desc: The data format simulates real loads. Controlled baseline: 256 B. 4K video: 500 KB. 8K video: 2 MB. Financial: small JSON messages. IoT: minimal high-frequency telemetry.

guide.tip: Start by selecting the platform to filter compatible options.

table.empty: No scenarios found. Create the first scenario to get started.
table.emptyCreate: Create one with the button above.

rabbitmqFinancialReliable.name: Reliable financial RabbitMQ
rabbitmqFinancialReliable.desc: AMQP queues for short transactions. This is the most defensible preset for observing latency and errors without huge payloads.

natsIotTelemetry.name: NATS IoT telemetry
natsIotTelemetry.desc: Lightweight pub/sub with minimal payload. This is the recommended NATS case before testing heavy loads.

kafka4kStreaming.name: Kafka 4K video log-centric
kafka4kStreaming.desc: Ordered log with an ephemeral topic per run. This is Kafka’s natural case for sustained throughput without forcing the 8K limit.

confluent8kStreaming.name: Confluent 8K video Kafka-compatible
confluent8kStreaming.desc: 2 MB payload through the Kafka-compatible path. This is the final 8K stress case for documenting message limits, backpressure and stability.

filters.title: Filters
filters.searchPlaceholder: Search by name, architecture, protocol, platform, or format

filters.title: Filters
filters.searchPlaceholder: Search by scenario, platform, protocol, architecture, or format

guide.title: Execution guide
guide.subtitle: What each status means, what you can do with a run and when to move to Results.

items.title: Statuses
items.text: Pending is waiting in the queue. The demo is limited to 3 concurrent executions because there are 3 nodes and we want to avoid too much Job contention. Running sends load; completed, stopped and failed are final states.

items.title: Run detail
items.text: Click a row to see configuration, timing, sent messages, received messages, stored measures, and diagnostics.

items.title: Actions
items.text: Stop keeps data that has already arrived. Delete removes the run and its samples. Reset all clears executions and measurements.

items.title: Move to Results
items.text: When the run finishes, Results reads the stored measurements and compares them without mixing repetitions.

confirm.messageStop: Are you sure you want to stop this execution?
confirm.messageDelete: Are you sure you want to delete this execution? The associated metric data will be lost.
confirm.messageDeleteSelected: Are you sure you want to delete the selected executions?
confirm.messageDeleteAll: Are you sure you want to delete all executions in this group?

execucions.subtitle: History of tests run on the AKS cluster.

summary.runningHelp: Runs that are sending load right now.

actions.resultsTitle: Open the historical comparison by scenario
actions.refreshTitle: Refresh executions and metrics without clearing the screen
actions.stopAllTitle: Stop all running or pending executions
actions.resetAllTitle: Delete all executions and samples from the cluster

detail.loadingSubtitle: Loading the saved samples for this run...
detail.subtitle: This panel summarizes the configuration, saved samples, and processed message volume.
detail.statReceivedHelper: Volume received by the consumer or recorded in the final telemetry.
detail.statSentHelper: Volume sent by the load-generator during this execution.
detail.statDurationHelper: Elapsed time for the visible run.
detail.interpretText: If the live area goes back to zero when you run a scenario again, that is expected: it is a new run and counters start over. When it finishes or is stopped manually, saved samples move to Results with their own runId.

resultats.heading: Results
resultats.subtitle: Live metrics and comparisons for async architectures.
resultats.beforeCompareText: First filter by data format to compare equivalent cases. The score only ranks visible results: if you change filters, it can change. Also inspect latency, throughput, and errors.

glossary.title: How to interpret this page
glossary.shortSubtitle: What messages, measurements and score mean.
glossary.heading: Quick glossary
glossary.scoreDescription: The score orders the visible scenarios from best to worst. It combines latency, throughput, errors and resources into a single value to ease comparison.

live.empty: No active executions at this moment.
live.emptySearch: No execution matches the current search.
live.emptyDescription: Start a scenario from the Scenarios page. You will see latency, throughput, P50, and P99 here when samples arrive.
live.metricsIntro: The top counter shows accumulated messages for the selected run. Charts and table show samples published by the load-generator every 5 seconds; the screen polls the API every 3 seconds. When you switch execution, counters belong only to that run.
live.noSamplesYet: This run does not have stored samples yet. It may be waiting for resources, have a pending pod, use an older load-generator image, or metrics-api may be rejecting POST requests.
live.p50Description: 50% of messages arrive below this time.
live.p99Description: 99% of messages arrive below this time.

history.searchPlaceholder: Search by scenario, platform, protocol or format
history.emptyMessage: Run scenarios to see the comparisons here.
history.emptyFilter: No results match the current filters.
history.statsTelemetryVisible: Telemetry points accumulated across visible executions
history.comparisonDesc: Choose a table row to compare it with the best visible scenario.
history.tableSubtitle: All executed scenarios with key metrics.
history.tableHint: Click a row to open the expanded scenario detail.

drawer.title: Scenario detail
drawer.subtitle: Summary of the visible executions for the same scenario.
drawer.statMeasuresHelper: Persisted points from all visible executions.
drawer.statSentHelper: Total volume sent by the load generator.
drawer.statScoreHelper: Relative to the visible scenarios under the current filters.
drawer.runsDescription: Each row is a run of the same scenario. The top row uses a weighted average: a run with more messages counts more.

score.title: Score
score.formula: We take each metric, convert it to a common scale, and multiply it by its weight. Then we subtract detected problems.
score.stepValue: First we convert each value to a common scale: 1 is very good and 0 is very bad.
score.stepWeight: Then we multiply that value by the metric weight. Example: 0.57 x 20% = 11.4 points.
score.stepDiscount: At the end we apply the discount for errors or message loss, if any.
